documents.title: F-1 Employment Authorization — Official
documents.summary: All employment for F-1 students is prohibited unless explicitly authorized in advance by the DSO and/or USCIS. Unauthorized work is a severe status violation. Different work types have distinct rules, application windows, and limitations.

scope.failureImpact: SEVIS termination, deportation, future visa ineligibility, scholarship revocation, permanent bar from U.S. re-entry

corePrinciples: Employment Principle 1: No work without explicit authorization
Principle 2: Authorization must precede employment start
Principle 3: Hours limitations are strictly enforced
Principle 4: DSO consultation is required for all employment decisions

procedures.title: On-Campus Employment Authorization Process
procedures.description: Process for obtaining and maintaining on-campus employment authorization

steps.details: Check with DSO that SEVIS record shows 'Active' status

steps.action: Obtain job offer from on-campus employer
steps.details: Must be from department listed in university's approved on-campus employers

steps.action: Submit employment authorization form to DSO
steps.details: Form I-9 and departmental approval required
steps.warning: Do not begin work until DSO approval is received

requirements: Social Security Number or application receipt

details: Maximum 20 hours/week during academic sessions; full-time during official breaks
Work must be physically on-campus or at an off-campus location educationally affiliated with the school
Employment begins only after classes start and SEVIS registration is complete
Must cease on-campus work if student withdraws or transfers
mitigation: DSO consultation before accepting any offer
Time-tracking and self-auditing
Immediate reporting of employment to DSO for SEVIS update

commonViolations.text: Working more than 20 hours during academic term
Beginning work before classes start

details: CPT must be an integral part of the established curriculum (internship, practicum, co-op)
Requires a specific course for credit or a mandatory degree requirement
Must have DSO authorization and a new I-20 with CPT notation before work begins
Full-time CPT for 12 months or more eliminates eligibility for post-completion OPT

commonViolations.text: Beginning work before I-20 with CPT is issued
Working beyond dates authorized on I-20
CPT for a job unrelated to major field of study

controls: Apply for CPT at least 10 business days before intended start date
Ensure job offer letter meets DSO requirements
Verify CPT authorization dates match employment dates exactly
mitigation: Submit complete CPT application package 15 days before start date
Maintain communication with academic advisor throughout CPT
Report any employment changes to DSO within 5 days

details: Pre-completion OPT: Limited to 20 hours/week while school is in session
Post-completion OPT: Full-time after degree completion
Requires USCIS Form I-765 filing fee and adjudication (90+ day processing)
Maximum 12 months of standard OPT per educational level
Unemployment limit: 90 days total during standard OPT
failureModes: Starting work before receiving EAD card
Failure to report employment or address changes to DSO within 10 days
mitigation: Apply for OPT up to 90 days before program end date, no later than 60 days after
Maintain detailed records of all employment (I-983 for STEM OPT)
Set calendar alerts for reporting deadlines and unemployment day counter

details: Eligibility: Degree in DHS STEM-designated program, employer enrolled in E-Verify
Requires formal training plan (Form I-983) signed by employer and student
Must apply before initial 12-month OPT EAD expires
Unemployment limit: 150 days total (including initial OPT period)
Mandatory self-evaluations and employer reporting to DSO every 6 months

commonViolations.text: Working for a non-E-Verify employer
Failing to submit timely I-983 evaluations
Material changes to training plan without DSO approval

controls: Confirm employer's E-Verify number before accepting offer
Submit STEM OPT application 90-100 days before current EAD expiry
Schedule bi-annual reporting reminders in calendar

details: Available only due to severe, unforeseen economic circumstances beyond student's control
Requires demonstration of efforts to find on-campus work and severe need
Must file Form I-765 with USCIS based on DSO recommendation and evidence
Authorization is for off-campus work, up to 20 hours/week during sessions
mitigation: Immediate consultation with DSO upon financial crisis
Document all circumstances (currency devaluation, sponsor loss, medical emergency)
Explore all other financial aid options first

details: Available if employment is with a recognized international organization (e.g., UN, IMF, World Bank)
Requires Form I-765 filing with USCIS and DSO recommendation

DSO.name: Designated School Official (DSO)
DSO.description: Contact your university's International Student Services Office (ISSO). Search '[your university name] international student office' to find their direct contact page, email, and appointment booking.

Employment_Office.name: Student Employment Center
Employment_Office.description: Contact your university's Student Employment Center or Career Services office. Search '[your university name] student employment' or '[your university name] career center' to find their direct contact page.

links.title: SEVP Policy Guidance: Practical Training
links.description: Official DHS guidance on F-1 employment

links.title: DHS STEM Designated Degree Program List

forms: Form I-765: Application for Employment Authorization
I-983 Training Plan for STEM OPT

complianceNotes: MoHE scholarship recipients must obtain additional approval for any employment
All employment income must be reported to MoHE annually
Tax filing required for all employment income
Unauthorized employment is grounds for immediate scholarship termination

documents.title: I-20 Maintenance & Updates — Official
documents.summary: The Form I-20 is the legal document certifying F-1 status and must be accurate, valid, and updated for all status-impacting changes. Students are responsible for requesting updates through DSO; failure to maintain current I-20 constitutes status violation.

scope.failureImpact: Loss of legal status, inability to travel/re-enter, employment authorization invalidation, scholarship suspension

procedures.title: Personal Information Updates
procedures.description: Process for updating name, address, and contact information

steps.action: Log in to university student portal

steps.details: Include street address, apartment number, city, state, ZIP
steps.warning: PO Box alone is not sufficient

procedures.title: Program Extension Request Process
procedures.description: Steps to extend I-20 before expiration date

steps.details: Obtain letter explaining need for extension
Updated bank statements showing sufficient funds
Include: extension form, advisor letter, financial proof
steps.warning: Applications after expiration are not accepted

details: Legal name changes must be reported with supporting documents (passport, marriage certificate)
U.S. residential address changes must be reported within 10 days of moving
Emergency contact information must remain current in SEVIS and university systems
failureModes: I-20 name/passport mismatch causing travel denial
mitigation: Update address through university portal immediately upon move
Request updated I-20 after any legal name change
Quarterly review of all personal information in SEVIS

details: Required if unable to complete program by I-20 end date due to compelling academic or medical reasons
Must apply before current I-20 expires with DSO recommendation
Requires financial documentation showing continued support for extended period
Academic advisor letter explaining delay necessity is mandatory

commonViolations.text: Applying after I-20 expiration date
Insufficient financial documentation for extension period
Continuing studies with expired I-20

controls: Monitor program progress against I-20 end date quarterly
Initiate extension request 60 days before expiration
Maintain communication with academic advisor about completion timeline

details: Must be accepted to new school before initiating transfer
Must complete current term or obtain authorized early withdrawal
Transfer release date must be coordinated between old and new DSOs
Cannot begin classes at new school until transfer is complete in SEVIS
failureModes: Starting classes at new school before SEVIS transfer
Failure to obtain MoHE approval for transfer
Missing transfer release deadline (typically 60 days after program end)
mitigation: Obtain written MoHE approval before initiating transfer
Submit transfer request to current DSO 30+ days before intended start
Confirm SEVIS transfer completion with new DSO before registration

details: Travel signature on page 2 of I-20 must be less than 12 months old at re-entry (6 months for active OPT)
Signatures are valid for multiple entries within their validity period
Emergency travel signatures may be available but require justification
failureModes: Denied boarding by airline due to expired signature
CBP denial of entry at port of entry
Stranded outside U.S. requiring emergency DSO contact
mitigation: Check travel signature validity before booking any international travel
Request new signature if current one expires within 30 days of planned return
Carry all previous I-20s as backup documentation

details: Significant changes in funding source (new sponsor, loss of funding) must be reported
Updated financial documents showing ability to cover remaining program costs required
MoHE-sponsored students must report any changes to both DSO and MoHE
Changing educational level (Bachelor's to Master's) requires new I-20
Substantial change in major may require new I-20 and financial documentation
Change in thesis/dissertation topic that substantially alters program may require update

DSO.name: Designated School Official (DSO) — SEVIS Records
DSO.description: Contact your university's International Student Services Office (ISSO) for I-20 updates and SEVIS record changes. Search '[your university name] international student office' to find their direct contact page, email, and appointment booking.

links.title: SEVP Policy Guidance: Maintaining Status
links.description: Official DHS guidance on status maintenance

documents.title: Grace Periods & Status Termination — Official
documents.summary: Grace periods provide limited lawful presence after program completion or status loss. Understanding triggers for status termination and restoration procedures is critical to avoid unlawful presence accrual.

scope.failureImpact: Unlawful presence accrual, 3/10-year re-entry bars, scholarship forfeiture, deportation proceedings

timeline.T0: Program completion date or status violation
timeline.T+15 days: DSO must report termination in SEVIS after violation
timeline.T+0 days (Violation): Unlawful presence begins immediately upon termination

details: 60-day period to depart U.S., transfer schools, or change status after program completion
Program completion date is earlier of: final exam date, thesis defense date, or I-20 end date
Cannot work during grace period except if on authorized OPT
Travel outside U.S. during grace period ends the period; cannot re-enter
failureModes: Attempting to re-enter during grace period
Beginning new program after grace period expires
mitigation: Mark calendar with exact grace period end date
Initiate transfer/change of status applications well before grace period ends
Confirm departure flight booked before grace period expiration

details: 30-day period for travel and departure after program end date on DS-2019
Cannot work or study during this period
Time cannot be extended for any reason
Failure to maintain full course load without authorization
Unauthorized employment (including unpaid internships)
Failure to report address change within 10 days
Failure to extend I-20/DS-2019 before expiration
Failure to enroll in classes by program start date
Substantial violation of school conduct policies leading to dismissal

commonViolations.text: Dropping below full-time without DSO approval
Working on-campus more than 20 hours/week
Forgetting to request program extension

details: Available if: violation was beyond student's control, student intends to complete studies, has not worked illegally, and is not deportable
Must file Form I-539 with USCIS with supporting evidence and fee
Processing time 6-12 months; cannot travel or work while application pending
Cannot apply if out of status more than 5 months unless exceptional circumstances
failureModes: USCIS denial leading to accrual of unlawful presence
Applying after 5-month deadline without exceptional circumstances
mitigation: Consult DSO immediately upon discovering status violation
Document all evidence showing violation was beyond control
Consider departing U.S. and re-entering with new I-20 if eligible

details: More restrictive than F-1; requires sponsoring agency recommendation
Must apply within 30 days of status violation discovery
Requires showing violation was minor or technical and beyond scholar's control
Departing U.S. after status termination stops unlawful presence accrual
May apply for new visa after departure but must disclose previous violation
Re-entry bar periods: 180 days unlawful presence = 3-year bar; 365+ days = 10-year bar

documents.title: Travel & Re-entry Rules — Official
documents.summary: International travel while maintaining F-1/J-1 status requires specific documentation validation and timing considerations. Re-entry is never guaranteed and is at CBP discretion. Advance preparation is mandatory.

scope.failureImpact: Denied entry to U.S., SEVIS termination, stranded outside U.S., scholarship interruption

timeline.Within 10 days: Report return to DSO if required

details: Valid passport (6+ months validity for most countries, including Oman)
Valid U.S. visa stamp (except for automatic revalidation cases)
Current I-20 with valid travel signature (F-1) or DS-2019 with travel validation (J-1)
Proof of financial support (current bank statements, sponsor letter)
Proof of enrollment/employment (transcript, enrollment verification, employment letter for OPT)
I-94 record from previous entry
failureModes: I-20 travel signature >12 months old (>6 months for OPT)
mitigation: Create travel document checklist and verify each item 72 hours before departure
Request new travel signatures if current will expire during travel
Carry both physical and digital copies of all documents

details: Must apply at U.S. Embassy/Consulate outside U.S., typically in home country (Oman)
Requires visa interview appointment and all supporting documents
Administrative processing (security checks) can add weeks/months to processing
Visa denial does not affect current status if still inside U.S.

commonViolations.text: Applying for visa in third country without strong ties
commonViolations.consequence: Visa denial, wasted time and money

commonViolations.text: Insufficient documentation of continued ties to home country
Misrepresenting employment or study plans

controls: Schedule visa appointment well in advance of planned travel
Prepare updated evidence of MoHE sponsorship and intent to return to Oman
Consult with DSO about timing and documentation before visa appointment

details: Allows re-entry with expired visa when traveling to contiguous territory (Canada, Mexico, adjacent islands) for <30 days
Requirements: Valid I-20/DS-2019 with travel signature, valid passport, valid I-94, not applying for new visa during trip
Not available if: from designated state sponsor of terrorism country (does not include Oman), previously denied visa, or out of status
Cannot visit other countries beyond contiguous territory during trip
failureModes: Applying for new visa while in contiguous territory
mitigation: Print and carry automatic revalidation regulations
Confirm all eligibility criteria before travel
Have DSO contact information available at port of entry

details: Must have: valid passport, valid visa (unless automatic revalidation), EAD card, I-20 with valid travel signature (<6 months old), job offer letter or employment verification
Unemployed OPT students may be denied re-entry if exceeding 90-day unemployment limit
STEM OPT requires I-20 with current employer information and valid travel signature
failureModes: Traveling while unemployed on OPT beyond 90-day limit
controls: Obtain employment verification letter before travel
Calculate unemployment days before departure
Carry multiple copies of EAD card separately

documents.title: J-1 Two-Year Home Residency Requirement — Official
documents.summary: Section 212(e) of the Immigration and Nationality Act mandates that certain J-1 exchange visitors return to their home country for two years after program completion before being eligible for certain U.S. immigration benefits. Determination is made at visa issuance.

scope.failureImpact: Ineligible for H/L/K visas, immigrant visas, or change of status to most nonimmigrant categories; must fulfill requirement or obtain waiver

details: Government-funded: Receiving funding from U.S. or home country government (including MoHE)
Skills list: Field appears on Exchange Visitor Skills List for home country (Oman maintains list)
Graduate medical education: Receiving medical training in U.S.
Check DS-2019 box 2 to see if subject to requirement
failureModes: Accepting barred immigration benefit and being denied
mitigation: Review DS-2019 box 2 upon receipt
Consult with J-1 program sponsor for clarification
Verify with MoHE if funding triggers requirement

details: Cannot change status inside U.S. to H, L, or K nonimmigrant categories
Cannot obtain immigrant visa or adjustment of status to permanent resident
Can return on B, F, or new J visa during 2-year period
Time outside home country does not count toward 2-year requirement

commonViolations.text: Applying for H-1B while subject to requirement
Marrying U.S. citizen and applying for adjustment without waiver

documents.title: Dependent Status (F-2/J-2) — Official
documents.summary: Spouses and unmarried children under 21 may accompany F-1/J-1 holders as dependents. Their status is derivative and contingent on principal's status. Specific rules govern their activities and benefits in U.S.

scope.failureImpact: Dependent status termination, unlawful presence accrual, separation from family, immigration violations

timeline.Within 30 days: School enrollment for children (varies by state)

details: Cannot work in any capacity, paid or unpaid
Can study part-time at SEVP-certified institutions (full-time requires change to F-1)
May engage in volunteer activities that are truly voluntary (not displacing paid workers)
Must maintain health insurance as required by university/policy
Status automatically terminates if F-1 principal loses status

commonViolations.text: F-2 spouse engaging in unpaid internship
F-2 child attending public school full-time without change of status

documents.title: Change of Status Procedures — Official
documents.summary: Changing from one nonimmigrant category to another (e.g., F-1 to H-1B) requires careful planning, timing, and adherence to specific procedures. Some changes can be done within U.S., others require consular processing. Unauthorized change attempts constitute status violation.

scope.failureImpact: Status gap leading to unlawful presence, employment authorization loss, deportation risk, future immigration benefit denial

timeline.T-90 days: File change of status application (varies by category)

details: H-1B petition must be filed while F-1 status is valid
Cap-gap provision automatically extends F-1 status and OPT work authorization until October 1 if selected
If not selected: Must depart by end of 60-day grace period or maintain other status
Change occurs on October 1 when H-1B status begins
failureModes: H-1B petition filed after OPT expiration
Traveling during cap-gap period without proper documentation

documents.title: Health Insurance & Medical Care — Official
documents.summary: All international students MUST maintain approved health insurance coverage. Understanding U.S. healthcare systems, emergency procedures, and insurance utilization is critical for health, safety, and financial protection. Uninsured medical expenses can lead to financial catastrophe.

scope.failureImpact: Medical debt, inability to access care, university registration hold, potential status violation

procedures.title: Medical Emergency Protocol
procedures.description: Steps to follow during medical emergencies

steps.details: Ambulance will transport to nearest emergency room
steps.warning: Do not drive yourself to hospital in emergency

details: Minimum coverage: $100,000 per accident/illness
Deductible maximum: $500 per policy year
Must include medical evacuation ($50,000) and repatriation ($25,000)
Mental health coverage must be included
Pre-existing conditions must be covered after 6-month waiting period

commonViolations.text: Allowing insurance to lapse
commonViolations.consequence: University registration hold, SEVIS termination risk

commonViolations.text: Using out-of-network providers without referral

mitigation: Set up auto-pay for insurance premiums
Verify network status before scheduling appointments
Carry insurance card at all times

details: U.S. prescriptions required for most medications
Bring 3-month supply of chronic medications from Oman with doctor's note
Schedule appointments with University Health Center for refills
Controlled substances have additional restrictions and monitoring
failureModes: Attempting to import controlled substances without proper documentation
controls: Schedule doctor appointment 30 days before medication runs out
Maintain original prescription containers with labels
Use single pharmacy for all prescriptions

University_Health_Center.name: Student Health Services
University_Health_Center.description: Contact your university's Student Health Services or Health Center. Search '[your university name] student health services' to find their direct contact page, hours, and appointment booking.

Insurance_Office.name: International Student Insurance
Insurance_Office.description: Contact your university's International Student Insurance or Benefits office. Search '[your university name] international student insurance' to find their direct contact and claim procedures.

Emergency_Mental_Health.name: Suicide and Crisis Lifeline
Emergency_Mental_Health.title: 24/7 Mental Health Crisis Support
Emergency_Mental_Health.description: Dial or text 988 — Suicide and Crisis Lifeline. Free, 24/7. Available in English and Spanish.

complianceNotes: MoHE-sponsored students must use university-approved insurance plan
All medical expenses over $500 must be pre-approved by MoHE
Keep detailed records of all medical visits and expenses
Annual physical examination required for continued insurance coverage

documents.title: Emergency Protocols & Crisis Management — Official
documents.summary: Preparedness for emergencies is critical for international students. Understanding emergency procedures, contact protocols, and support resources can mean the difference between successful resolution and catastrophic consequences.

scope.failureImpact: Physical harm, legal complications, status jeopardy, financial loss

procedures.title: Immediate Emergency Response
procedures.description: Actions to take in life-threatening situations

details: Must maintain current emergency contact information in university system
Local U.S. contact person recommended (fellow Omani student)
Omani embassy contact information must be readily accessible
DSO emergency contact information must be saved in phone
failureModes: Inability to reach contacts during crisis
mitigation: Update contacts each semester
Establish buddy system with other Omani students
Program emergency numbers into phone speed dial

University_Emergency.name: Campus Emergency Line
University_Emergency.description: Contact your university's campus safety or police department. Search '[your university name] campus police' or '[your university name] campus emergency' to find their 24/7 number.

DSO_Emergency.name: DSO After-Hours Emergency
DSO_Emergency.description: Contact your university's International Student Services Office for their after-hours emergency line. Search '[your university name] international student office emergency' or check their website for an after-hours number.

documents.title: Academic Integrity & Conduct — Official
documents.summary: U.S. academic institutions enforce strict standards of academic integrity. Violations can result in severe consequences including course failure, suspension, expulsion, and immigration status termination. Understanding these standards is essential for academic success.

scope.failureImpact: Academic penalties, suspension/expulsion, SEVIS termination, scholarship revocation

details: Copying text without quotation marks and citation
Paraphrasing without proper attribution
Using another student's work (current or previous)
Purchasing or commissioning papers from any source
Self-plagiarism (submitting same work for multiple courses)

commonViolations.text: Copying from online sources without citation
Collaboration on individual assignments
commonViolations.consequence: Zero on assignment, reporting to honor board

commonViolations.text: Using translation software for language assignments

mitigation: Attend academic integrity workshops
Use citation management software
Consult writing center for proper citation methods
Submit work through plagiarism checkers

details: No unauthorized materials in exam room
Electronic devices prohibited unless explicitly allowed
No communication with other students during exam
Must follow specific seating arrangements
Time limits are strictly enforced
failureModes: Continuing to write after time is called
controls: Clear desk of all materials before exam
Ask proctor about allowed items before exam starts
Set watch to exam time for self-monitoring

Academic_Integrity_Office.name: Office of Academic Integrity
Academic_Integrity_Office.description: Contact your university's Office of Academic Integrity or Dean of Students. Search '[your university name] academic integrity office' to find their direct contact page, reporting procedures, and appointment booking.

Writing_Center.name: University Writing Center
Writing_Center.description: Contact your university's Writing Center or Academic Support office. Search '[your university name] writing center' to find their hours, location, and appointment booking for citation and research help.

complianceNotes: MoHE requires reporting of any academic integrity violations
Suspension for academic dishonesty may trigger scholarship repayment
Cultural differences in collaboration should be discussed with professors early
Language assistance is available through writing center, not through unauthorized help

documents.title: Cultural Adaptation & Support — Advisory
documents.summary: Successful cultural adaptation enhances academic success and personal wellbeing. Understanding cultural differences, accessing support resources, and building community connections are essential components of the international student experience.

scope.failureImpact: Social isolation, academic difficulties, mental health challenges

procedures.title: Building Community Connections
procedures.description: Steps to establish support network in new environment

steps.details: Monthly meetings, cultural events, peer support

details: Punctuality is highly valued in academic and professional settings
Direct communication is common; ask questions when unclear
Class participation often contributes to grades
Office hours are for student questions and relationship building
mitigation: Arrive 5-10 minutes early for all appointments
Practice asking questions in class settings
Schedule regular office hours with professors
Observe and learn from American classmates

details: Prayer space available in Interfaith Center (Room 305)
Halal food options in campus dining halls (labeled)
Ramadan accommodations available through DSO
Modest dress guidelines for labs and professional settings
controls: Register religious accommodations at beginning of semester
Meet with professors to discuss exam schedules during Ramadan
Utilize halal food advisory from Muslim Student Association

Omani_Student_Association.name: Omani Student Association (OSA)
Omani_Student_Association.description: Contact your campus Omani Student Association or International Students Association. Ask the International Student Services office for current OSA contact information.

International_Student_Life.name: International Student Programs
International_Student_Life.description: Contact your university's International Student Life or International Student Programs office. Search '[your university name] international student life' to find cultural adjustment support and events.

Muslim_Student_Association.name: Muslim Student Association (MSA)
Muslim_Student_Association.description: Contact your campus Muslim Student Association for prayer space and halal food information. Search '[your university name] Muslim Student Association' or ask the International Student Services office.

links.title: Omani Cultural Attaché Student Support
links.description: Resources specifically for Omani students in US

complianceNotes: Cultural adjustment challenges are normal and expected
Seeking help is a sign of strength, not weakness
MoHE provides additional cultural support through embassy contacts
Maintaining Omani cultural identity while adapting to US norms is encouraged

documents.title: Banking, Money Management, and Financial Setup — US
documents.summary: Opening a US bank account is a first-week priority — most traditional banks require a Social Security Number (SSN), but several have international student programmes that allow account opening with a passport and I-20 alone. Wise (formerly TransferWise) is the most cost-effective way to convert Omani Rial (OMR) to US Dollars and receive your MoHE stipend. Building a US credit history from day one matters: a good credit score is required for apartment rentals, mobile phone plans, and many financial services.

appliesTo: All Omani students arriving in the US
MoHE-sponsored scholars managing living allowances in the US
scope.failureImpact: Inability to receive stipend, difficulty renting housing, limited access to US financial services

details: Most US banks require a Social Security Number (SSN) to open an account — however, several major banks have international student programmes that accept a passport, I-20, and university enrollment letter instead

Chase College Checking: one of the strongest international student options — many branches open accounts for F-1 students without an SSN using passport and I-20; no monthly fee with a linked college account
Bank of America Campus Checking: available at partner universities; some branches can open accounts without SSN — bring your passport, I-20, and a university acceptance or enrollment letter
Wells Fargo: student accounts available; SSN requirement varies by branch — call your local branch before visiting to confirm their international student policy

Wise (borderless USD account): the fastest option if you need to receive money before your US bank account is open — Wise gives you US account details (routing and account number) that work with most US payment systems; ideal for receiving your first MoHE stipend transfer
Documents typically required: valid passport, F-1 or J-1 visa stamp, I-20 or DS-2019, proof of US address (a university accommodation letter suffices on arrival), university enrollment letter
Apply for your bank account in the first week — you will need it for housing payments, stipend deposits, and on-campus employment
authorizationSources: Individual bank policies — verify current requirements directly with your bank branch
controls: Visit the bank branch closest to your university in week one — bring all documents listed above
If you are rejected due to SSN requirements, open a Wise account immediately as a bridge until you have a US bank account
Enable two-factor authentication on your online banking from day one

details: SSN (Social Security Number): F-1 students cannot apply for an SSN unless they have authorised employment — on-campus employment or CPT/OPT qualifies

To apply for an SSN: obtain a job offer letter from your employer (on-campus department) and a letter from your DSO confirming your employment authorisation, then visit your local Social Security Administration (SSA) office in person
SSN applications can be filed no earlier than 30 days before your employment start date

ITIN (Individual Taxpayer Identification Number): for students who need to file a US tax return but are not eligible for an SSN — used only for tax purposes, not for work authorisation or identity verification
Apply for an ITIN using IRS Form W-7, submitted with your tax return — your international student tax services centre or DSO office can advise
Many F-1 students on MoHE scholarships (no work income) may not need either an SSN or ITIN in their first academic year — confirm with your DSO and university's tax support office
Once you have an SSN, update your bank account records — this can unlock additional account features and credit products
authorizationSources: IRS Publication 519 (US Tax Guide for Aliens)
controls: Never pay anyone to 'get you an SSN faster' — SSN applications are free and processed only by the Social Security Administration
Confirm with your DSO before applying: premature or incorrect SSN applications can create complications with SEVIS

details: MoHE living allowances are disbursed monthly or quarterly — confirm your payment schedule and transfer method before departure from Oman
MoHE typically transfers funds in Omani Rial (OMR) via international wire — the most cost-effective way to receive these in the US is via a Wise or Remitly transfer to your USD account
Build a 4–6 week emergency cash buffer before you arrive: international bank transfers to the US typically take 3–5 business days and may be held by US banks for 5–10 business days on first receipt

Typical monthly US student living costs (2025 estimates): major cities (New York, Boston, Los Angeles, San Francisco) USD $2,500–$4,000; mid-size cities (Houston, Phoenix, Chicago, Seattle) USD $1,500–$2,500; smaller college towns USD $1,000–$1,800 — covering rent, food, transport, phone; not tuition
Halal food is available in major US cities through specialty grocery stores, halal butchers, and restaurant chains — budget may be slightly higher if purchasing from specialist retailers
MoHE allows a temporary increase in allowance for high-cost-of-living cities in some cases — contact your MoHE scholarship officer if your stipend does not cover basic living costs in your city
controls: Bring sufficient funds (cash or a pre-loaded travel debit card) to cover at least 4–6 weeks of expenses on arrival
Notify your US bank in advance when expecting a large international wire — banks can place holds or freeze accounts on unexpected large incoming transfers
Set up a Wise account to convert OMR to USD at interbank rates and reduce conversion fees

details: Wise (formerly TransferWise): the most widely recommended service for OMR to USD transfers — uses mid-market exchange rates with a transparent fee, typically far cheaper than bank-to-bank SWIFT transfers; transfers usually arrive in 1–3 business days
Remitly: a strong alternative to Wise for transfers to US bank accounts from Oman — competitive rates and mobile-friendly

Bank-to-bank SWIFT wire transfers: available from Bank Muscat, NBO, and other Omani banks, but fees of USD $25–50 per transfer and 3–5 business day delivery make them the most expensive option for regular allowance transfers
Western Union and MoneyGram: available but generally have less favourable exchange rates for the amounts typical of student allowances

US banks are required to report deposits over USD $10,000 under anti-money-laundering regulations — this is routine reporting, not an accusation; ensure any large transfers are documented as MoHE scholarship disbursements
Keep records of all international transfers — your MoHE scholarship officer may request evidence of allowance receipt
authorizationSources: FDIC-insured financial institutions and FinCEN-regulated transfer services

details: The US has three major credit bureaus: Experian, Equifax, and TransUnion — your credit score is calculated from the activity they record; most international students arrive with no US credit history
No credit history means landlords and phone carriers often require larger deposits or guarantors — start building credit in your first semester

Secured credit cards are the most accessible credit-building tool for international students: Discover It Secured and Capital One Secured Mastercard do not require an SSN to apply (passport acceptable) and report to all three bureaus

How a secured card works: you deposit USD $200–$500 as collateral, receive a card with that credit limit, use it for small monthly purchases (groceries, subscriptions), and pay the full balance every month — no interest charges, and your credit score grows
Never carry a balance on a credit card: the interest rates on US credit cards (18–28% APR) will quickly exceed any reward benefits
Credit-building services: some banks (including Bank of America and Chase) offer credit-builder loans for students — ask about options when opening your account
After 6–12 months of on-time payments, your credit score will typically reach 680–720 — sufficient for most apartment leases without a cosigner
controls: Apply for a secured credit card within your first month — Discover It Secured is fully available to F-1 students with a passport (SSN not required at application)
Set up automatic payment of the full statement balance each month — never pay just the minimum
Monitor your credit report at annualcreditreport.com (free, once per year per bureau) — dispute any errors immediately

details: IRS impersonation scams: the most common scam in the US — fraudsters call or email claiming to be the IRS and threatening arrest, deportation, or visa cancellation if you do not pay immediately by wire transfer or gift card. The IRS does NOT call students demanding immediate payment; they contact you by post. Hang up.

USCIS/immigration fee scams: fraudulent websites or callers posing as USCIS charge fees for status checks, OPT applications, or visa extensions that are free or processed directly through official channels — always use uscis.gov

Scholarship or visa 'consultant' scams: services that claim to guarantee scholarships, visas, or CPT/OPT approval for a fee — these are fraudulent; immigration advice must come from a licensed attorney or your university DSO
Cheque overpayment scams: a 'client' sends you a cheque for more than agreed and asks you to return the difference by wire — the original cheque is fake; you lose the money you 'return'

Apartment rental scams: listings on Craigslist and Facebook Marketplace for apartments in university cities are frequently fraudulent — never wire a deposit or 'application fee' to someone you have not met in person, and never pay before viewing
If you think you have been scammed: report to the FTC (reportfraud.ftc.gov) and your university's international student office immediately

complianceNotes: MoHE requires you to maintain a bank account in your own name for allowance disbursement — joint accounts or accounts in another person's name may not be accepted for stipend payments
Large unexplained cash deposits can trigger bank account freezes under US anti-money-laundering rules — always retain documentation showing that incoming transfers are your MoHE scholarship allowance
Opening a US bank account does not affect your F-1 immigration status — banking is a routine part of lawful student life in the US
If you take on-campus employment and obtain an SSN, update your bank records — an SSN unlocks credit card, loan, and investment account options unavailable without one
